contents:
n the chart above, we can see that utility stocks barely budged during the last two sell-offs, and have continued to rise throughout the Brexit fiasco.

Some investors may think that because everything is returning to normal, utility stocks must erase their earlier gains. This conclusion isn't completely unfounded, since there has been a flight to safety, which temporarily boosted demand for utility stocks. However, it's important to understand that they have benefited fundamentally as well. A big headline we saw this week was that the 30-year Treasury rate has declined to historic lows. It has since bounced back a bit to 2.24% from 2.14%. Lower yields should result in cheaper debt, leading to more cash for equity holders. But as economic uncertainty goes away, investors are concerned that when rates rebound, utility stocks will be on the chopping block. I disagree.
